meta.name: Tempura
meta.subtitle: Light, crisp Japanese batter-fry for shrimp and vegetables

source.name: ssa-core
notes: Cold batter + hot oil technique for airy, delicate crispness.
Designed to be modular: shrimp + mixed vegetables with optional tentsuyu dipping sauce.

yield.notes: Serves 4 as an appetizer or light main when paired with rice/noodles and soup.

signals.notes: Tempura is best eaten immediately. Re-crisping is possible but not ideal.

tasteProfile.balanceNotes: Tempura is mild and relies on dipping sauce (tentsuyu), salt, or citrus for contrast.

optional.label: Electric deep fryer
optional.notes: Helps maintain stable oil temperature for consistent results.

notes: For gluten-free tempura: use a gluten-free flour blend + rice flour; results vary.
For shellfish-free: omit shrimp; use only vegetables.

ingredients.label: Neutral frying oil (canola/avocado/peanut)
ingredients.notes: Amount depends on pot size; aim for 2–3 inches depth.

ingredients.label: Large shrimp, peeled and deveined (tails on optional)
ingredients.notes: About 12–16 large shrimp. Omit for vegetarian tempura.

ingredients.label: Salt
ingredients.notes: Lightly season shrimp/veg before frying or serve as finishing salt.

ingredients.label: Nori sheets (for optional nori tempura)
ingredients.notes: Cut into strips; fries very fast.

ingredients.label: All-purpose flour
ingredients.notes: About 1 cup. Keep cold if possible.

ingredients.label: Rice flour
ingredients.notes: About 1/2 cup. Helps crispness; can replace with more AP flour if needed.

ingredients.label: Cornstarch (or potato starch)
ingredients.notes: About 1/4 cup. Optional but improves lightness and crunch.

ingredients.label: Baking powder
ingredients.notes: Optional lift; classic tempura can omit leavener.

ingredients.label: Egg
ingredients.notes: Some tempura batters use egg; optional here for flexibility.

ingredients.label: Ice-cold water
ingredients.notes: About 1 1/4 cups. Must be very cold.

ingredients.label: Ice cubes
ingredients.notes: Use to keep batter cold while frying.

ingredients.label: Dashi (for tentsuyu dipping sauce)
ingredients.notes: About 1 cup. Use instant dashi or homemade.

steps.title: Set up the frying station
instructions: Place a wire rack over a sheet pan (or line a tray with paper towels).
Set out a spider/skimmer, chopsticks or tongs, and a thermometer.
Have a second tray with a light dusting of flour for dredging (optional but helpful).

steps.title: Prep shrimp and vegetables
instructions: Pat vegetables dry; excess moisture makes batter slip and oil spatter.
Slice sweet potato thin (1/8 inch) so it cooks through quickly.
Slice eggplant and zucchini; keep pieces similar size for even frying.
If using shrimp: pat dry. Make 3–4 shallow slashes on the belly side and gently press to straighten (prevents curling).

steps.title: Make tentsuyu dipping sauce (optional)
instructions: In a small saucepan, combine dashi, soy sauce, and mirin.
Bring to a gentle simmer for 2–3 minutes, then remove from heat.
Serve warm or room temperature with grated daikon (optional).

steps.title: Heat oil to frying temperature
instructions: Fill a heavy pot with 2–3 inches of oil (do not exceed half full).
Heat to 340–360°F (170–182°C). Aim for ~350°F (175°C) for most items.
Keep the thermometer clipped and adjust heat to maintain temperature.

steps.title: Mix the tempura batter (keep it cold, mix minimally)
instructions: In a bowl, whisk flour, rice flour (if using), cornstarch (if using), and baking powder (if using).
In a separate bowl or measuring cup, beat the egg with ice-cold water (or just use ice water if omitting egg).
Pour wet into dry and mix very lightly with chopsticks or a fork—leave small lumps. Overmixing develops gluten and makes batter heavy.
Set the bowl over ice (or add a few ice cubes) to keep batter cold while frying.
checks: Batter is cold and lightly mixed

steps.title: Fry in small batches
instructions: Optional: lightly dredge items in flour to help batter adhere; shake off excess.
Dip pieces into batter and let excess drip off.
Gently lay items into hot oil away from you. Do not overcrowd—temperature drops cause greasiness.
Fry until pale golden and crisp: vegetables ~2–4 minutes; shrimp ~1.5–2.5 minutes depending on size.
Use the spider to remove and drain on the rack. Sprinkle a pinch of salt immediately if desired.
Skim loose batter bits between batches to keep oil clean.
checks: Batter is crisp and light (not dark brown)

steps.title: Serve immediately
instructions: Arrange tempura on a platter.
Serve with tentsuyu dipping sauce and grated daikon (optional).
Offer lemon wedges and/or a small dish of finishing salt.

serveWith.label: Shredded cabbage salad with sesame dressing

pairings.notes: Tempura pairs well with simple broths/noodles and light, fresh sides.

refrigerator.notes: Store uncovered on a rack if possible to reduce sogginess; best eaten same day.

methods: Oven/toaster oven at 400°F / 205°C for 6–10 minutes on a rack
reheat.notes: Avoid microwave (softens batter). Re-crisping is decent but not like fresh.

planner.batchNotes: Plan to fry right before eating; prep vegetables and sauce ahead to reduce active time.

notes: Key success: keep batter cold and mix minimally; keep oil hot and fry in small batches.
If batter slides off: ingredients may be too wet or not dredged; pat dry and dust lightly with flour.
If tempura is greasy: oil too cool or pot overcrowded; let oil recover between batches.
If tempura is tough: batter overmixed; mix fewer strokes and accept lumps.
Use hearty vegetables like sweet potato, mushrooms, broccoli florets, asparagus, and shishitos.
Serve with lemon + salt or tentsuyu + grated daikon.
Omit egg; use only ice-cold water for the wet component.
Mix lightly; the batter will still crisp well.
Use more rice flour and/or potato starch; reduce AP flour accordingly.
Keep batter very cold and fry at the higher end of the temp range (360°F / 182°C).

summary: Crisp, airy Japanese tempura using a cold, lightly mixed batter and hot oil. Includes optional tentsuyu dipping sauce and flexible shrimp/vegetable mix.

oilSafety: Never fill the pot more than halfway with oil.
Keep a lid nearby to smother flames; do not use water on a grease fire.
Monitor temperature to avoid smoking oil.
handling: Pat ingredients dry to reduce splatter.
Lower food into oil away from you to prevent burns.
